summary: Jeff Atwood argues that verbose code is superior to concise code because code is written once but read many times. He uses examples from Monad (PowerShell), wget, and the VB.NET vs C# debate to illustrate how verbose, self-documenting syntax aids comprehension. Drawing heavily on Steve McConnell's Code Complete, he asserts that optimizing for write-time convenience over read-time convenience is a false economy. The post challenges the common criticism that VB.NET is 'too verbose' by reframing verbosity as a feature, not a bug.
key_points: Verbose command-line parameters (like --mirror vs -m) make code self-documenting and immediately understandable
The criticism that VB.NET is 'too verbose' treats verbosity as inherently negative when it's actually beneficial for readability
Saving keystrokes while writing code is a fool's economy since code is read far more often than it's written
Professional programmers write readable code regardless of whether they think they're the only ones who will read it
Studies show maintenance programmers spend 50-60% of their time just trying to understand existing code
money_quotes: People say VB.NET is too verbose like that's a bad thing.
Saving keystrokes while writing code is a fool's economy.
Is English too verbose? Would this post be easier to read in a court reporter's shorthand?
Making code readable is not an optional part of the development process, and favoring write-time convenience over read-time convenience is a false economy.
The idea of writing unreadable code because you're the only person working on a project sets a dangerous precedent.
A professional programmer writes readable code, period.
Habits affect all your work; you can't turn them on and off at will, so be sure that what you're doing is something that you want to become a habit.
key_insight: Code readability through verbosity isn't a luxury or personal preference—it's a professional obligation that pays dividends across the entire maintenance lifecycle.
